title: Houthis Open New Front on Saudi Shipping, Trump Hits Canada With 50% Tariffs, and Defense Supply Chains Get Tighter
subhead: Yemen's Houthis declared a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia targeting the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, threatening 7% of global oil supply on top of the existing Hormuz disruption. Trump imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods under an obscure 1930 trade law. The ECB meets Thursday with oil-driven inflation back in play.

sections.heading: Houthis Declare Naval Blockade on Saudi Arabia, Threatening Bab el-Mandeb
sections.body:
Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis declared a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia on Monday, opening a second chokepoint in global oil flows already reeling from the near-total closure of the Strait of Hormuz since March. The group said the embargo was 'effective immediately' and based on 'an eye for an eye,' citing Saudi Arabia's role in the Yemen conflict and last week's Saudi strikes on Sanaa airport.

According to shipping analytics firm Kpler, 7.4 million barrels per day of petroleum transited the Bab el-Mandeb Strait in June, up from 4.2 million bpd a year earlier, as Saudi Arabia redirected crude exports from Hormuz to its Red Sea terminal at Yanbu. UBS analyst Giovanni Staunovo told Al-Monitor that the kingdom has increased Yanbu exports to around 4.5 million bpd, more than 70% of Saudi crude shipments. Of those, roughly 2.5 million bpd pass south through Bab el-Mandeb toward Asia, according to Rystad Energy.

Rystad's Jorge Leon noted that the Houthis have not explained how they intend to enforce the blockade, but previous attacks on commercial vessels demonstrate both capability and willingness. Reuters reported on July 16 that Iran has been urging the Houthis to close Bab el-Mandeb if the US continues striking Iranian power infrastructure.

Brent crude traded above $88 per barrel on Monday, with prices briefly spiking above $90 on the combined Hormuz and Houthi threat, before softening on Tuesday on reports of mediation efforts. Oil had surged past $90 earlier in the week after Iran closed Hormuz following the ninth consecutive night of US airstrikes.
sections.whyItMatters: The Houthi blockade threatens to shut down the alternate route Saudi Arabia has been using to bypass Hormuz. If both chokepoints are effectively closed, roughly 25-30% of global oil supply would be disrupted, with no easy rerouting alternatives left.

sections.heading: US-Iran Escalation: 10th Consecutive Night of Strikes, Tanker Hit in Hormuz
sections.body:
The US military carried out its 10th consecutive night of strikes on Iran early Tuesday, targeting Iranian military command centers, air defense systems, maritime capabilities, and missile and drone launch sites, according to US Central Command. The strikes followed Iran's attacks on US-allied states — Kuwait, Jordan, and Bahrain — and the death of another American service member in Jordan.

A commercial tanker was hit by a projectile in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, forcing its crew to abandon ship, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations center. It was at least the third tanker attack in 24 hours in the strait, with Iran's Revolutionary Guard claiming responsibility for earlier assaults.

Iran's Interior Minister traveled to Pakistan for talks with a key mediator, offering a glimmer of diplomatic progress. But the interim deal signed last month to end the fighting has effectively crumbled, with neither side showing signs of de-escalation on the ground. Nearly 100 US service members have been injured since strikes resumed on July 7, according to Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell.
sections.whyItMatters: The war is no longer contained to US-Iran bilateral strikes — it has expanded to involve direct attacks on Gulf states and commercial shipping across two critical waterways. Each escalation cycle narrows the window for a diplomatic off-ramp.

sections.heading: Trump Slaps 50% Tariffs on Canada Under Obscure 1930 Trade Law
sections.body:
President Trump on Monday imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, invoking Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act — a provision that the Cato Institute's Scott Lincicome called 'the nuclear option for Trump tariffs.' The tariffs exclude energy products, potash, fish, and critical minerals, but include goods previously protected by the USMCA, which the US chose not to renew.

The White House said the tariffs take effect in 30 days, leaving a window for negotiations. Trump signed three proclamations claiming Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol, and dairy products. However, Canada's retaliatory tariffs cited by Trump were themselves responses to earlier US tariffs imposed under the pretext of fentanyl enforcement.

Ontario Premier Doug Ford called for 'tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar' retaliation. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce urged both sides to use the 30-day window for meaningful negotiations. Several Democratic lawmakers had previously warned that Section 338 could be used to destabilize the economy.

The move comes amid the broader USMCA renegotiation process and threatens to cascade into a full-scale North American trade war. Mexico's economy was already forecast to grow more slowly than expected due to trade concerns with the US, according to a Reuters poll published the same day.
sections.whyItMatters: Using Section 338 — an almost never-invoked provision — signals that the administration is willing to bypass normal trade procedures entirely. Canada is the US's largest trading partner. A 50% tariff wall would be inflationary for both economies and fracture North American supply chains at a time when the US is also pressing defense supply chain onshoring.

sections.heading: ECB Meets Thursday as Oil-Driven Inflation Resurfaces
sections.body:
The European Central Bank meets this Thursday with markets pricing in a hold at 2.25%, but the calculus has shifted significantly since the last meeting. Eurozone inflation was confirmed at 2.8% for June on Friday — the first decline this year — and an ECB survey released Monday showed firms expect moderating wage growth and selling prices. But Brent crude back above $88 has reignited inflation concerns.

German two-year bond yields climbed to a two-year high on Monday as markets began pricing in two ECB rate hikes by early 2027, according to Reuters. The ECB was the first major central bank to raise rates after the Iran war began in February, hiking in June. Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem has explicitly warned that 'consecutive' rate hikes are possible if oil-driven inflation persists. Canada's June CPI came in at 2.8%, below expectations, but the BoC flagged upside risks from energy.

Berenberg economist Felix Schmidt told AFP that a hold this week is the most likely outcome, noting oil prices have 'not jumped too sharply' and there are limited signs of second-round inflation effects. ING's Carsten Brzeski said the meeting 'promises one last clash between the hawks and doves' before summer recess. The ECB will not have new forecasts this week — those come in September, when many analysts expect another hike.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB's June hike was already controversial. With oil re-surging and Hormuz disrupted, the central bank faces an uncomfortable choice: hold and risk inflation expectations de-anchoring, or hike into a eurozone economy that was already struggling before the energy shock.

sections.heading: Trump Tightens Defense Supply Chain Waivers, Targeting China Dependency
sections.body:
President Trump signed an executive order on Monday making it significantly harder for US defense contractors to obtain waivers allowing them to buy critical minerals and components from China and other prohibited foreign suppliers. White House adviser Peter Navarro told reporters the new standard ends 'we tried nothing and we're out of options' excuses from contractors.

The order requires companies seeking waivers to prove they searched for alternatives, explain their supply chain origins, and present a plan to transition away from prohibited suppliers. Contractors face loss of contracts if they cannot demonstrate sufficient onshoring efforts. The order also mandates comprehensive supply chain mapping from raw materials through finished military products, extending government visibility beyond prime contractors to lower-tier suppliers.

Separately, the White House announced an order targeting aluminum imports. Defense News reported that the moves come as the Pentagon pushes Lockheed Martin and Boeing to rapidly expand weapons production while dealing with persistent supply chain vulnerabilities — many critical minerals and processed materials used in missiles, aircraft, and advanced systems still rely on Chinese suppliers.

Navarro framed the order as 'battlefield preparation,' arguing the Pentagon needs to know whether weapons systems depend on foreign-controlled suppliers before a conflict begins.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not symbolic. Defense contractors like Lockheed and Boeing now face hard deadlines to reshore critical mineral supply chains — a process that takes years and billions of dollars. During an active conflict requiring expanded weapons production, the order creates a direct tension between supply chain purity and output volume.

sections.heading: South Korea Advances CBDC to Live Government Transactions, as US Blocks Digital Dollar
sections.body:
The Bank of Korea announced Monday that it will begin large-scale live transactional testing of its digital won with nine commercial banks this September, deploying real government subsidy money through a permissioned blockchain for the first time. The test will route approximately 30 billion won in EV charging infrastructure subsidies through the Digital Currency System (DCS), using tokenized deposits with smart contracts that enforce spending conditions at the point of transaction.

The system uses a two-layer 'burn-and-issue' mechanism drawn from the Bank for International Settlements' unified ledger concept. When a customer pays a merchant at a different bank, deposit tokens are burned at the sending bank, wholesale central bank currency transfers interbank, and new tokens are issued at the receiving bank — all as a single atomic transaction, eliminating settlement risk. Governor Shin Hyun-song presented the architecture at the ECB Forum on Central Banking 2026.

The contrast with the US is stark: Washington is still debating whether to ban a digital dollar through 2030. South Korea is moving from pilot to live government transactions, and the technical architecture — built on Hyperledger Besu with Proof of Authority consensus — is designed specifically to preserve the 'singleness of money' that private stablecoins cannot guarantee.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea is now the most advanced major economy in deploying a CBDC for real government payments. The burn-and-issue design addresses the core vulnerability that doomed earlier digital currency experiments: settlement risk. If successful, it creates a template that other central banks will follow — and a payments infrastructure that could reduce dependence on US-dollar-based settlement rails.

sections.heading: Gold Holds Near $4,000 as Safe-Haven Demand Collides With Rising Yields
sections.body:
Gold traded back above $4,000 per ounce on Monday, with spot prices reaching $4,015 according to USA Today, before easing slightly as rising Treasury yields and a stronger dollar capped gains. The metal had suffered a 14% decline during Q2 — its weakest quarterly performance since 2013 — but the renewed escalation in the US-Iran conflict has driven fresh safe-haven demand.

The dollar index (DXY) rose 0.17-0.18% on Monday as investors sought safety, putting downward pressure on gold even as geopolitical risk supported it. Market analysts note that the US Federal Reserve's hawkish stance — with higher-for-longer rates — creates a headwind for bullion, even as war-driven uncertainty provides a floor.

Oil prices softened slightly on Tuesday as reports of mediation efforts between the US and Iran offset supply disruption fears. Brent crude traded above $88 after briefly crossing $90, while US regular gasoline averaged $4 per gallon. The interplay between energy inflation, Fed rate expectations, and geopolitical risk continues to define the gold-oil-dollar triangle.
sections.whyItMatters: Gold above $4,000 signals persistent geopolitical risk premium, but the fact it has struggled to break sustainantly higher despite a two-front oil disruption (Hormuz + Bab el-Mandeb) shows how much the Fed's hawkish stance is capping the trade. The real test comes if the ECB hikes this week and the Fed signals further tightening.

bottomLine: The Houthi blockade of Saudi shipping through Bab el-Mandeb threatens the last viable exit route for Gulf oil, compounding the Hormuz disruption. If both chokepoints are effectively sealed, roughly a quarter of global supply is at risk. Trump's 50% Canada tariffs under Section 338 — a nearly unprecedented use of a 1930 law — open a new North American trade front just as the ECB debates whether oil inflation warrants another rate hike. Defense supply chain tightening and South Korea's CBDC advance signal that the financial infrastructure of great power competition is being rebuilt in real time.
sources: Al-Monitor, Reuters, Business Standard, Defense News, White House Fact Sheet, The Hindu Business Line, Free Malaysia Today, EconoTimes, CNBC, USA Today, UBS, Rystad Energy, Kpler, Bank of Korea, FXEmpire, Cato Institute, Berenberg, ING, Moneycontrol, TechCrunch/TechTimes, CoinDesk, Financial Express
